options.text: SGD (+momentum) is simple and can generalize well with a tuned schedule but converges slower; Adam adapts per-parameter learning rates for fast, robust default training and is standard (AdamW) for transformers

options.label: C
options.text: SGD requires no learning rate tuning at all, unlike Adam

options.label: D
options.text: Adam and SGD are mathematically identical, just named differently

explanation: SGD (+momentum): simple, often best final generalization with a tuned LR schedule; slower to converge. Adam: per-parameter adaptive learning rates from 1st/2nd moment estimates; fast, robust default (lr=1e-3), standard for transformers (AdamW variant decouples weight decay). Senior point: AdamW is the transformer default; SGD still common in vision.

title: Q78. Write a custom PyTorch Dataset + DataLoader.
description:
Q78. Write a custom PyTorch Dataset + DataLoader.

```python
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader
class CSVDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, X, y):
        self.X = torch.tensor(X, dtype=torch.float32)
        self.y = torch.tensor(y, dtype=torch.long)
    def __len__(self): return len(self.y)
    def __getitem__(self, i): return self.X[i], self.y[i]

loader = DataLoader(CSVDataset(X, y), batch_size=64,
                    shuffle=True, num_workers=4)
```

options.label: A
options.text: `__init__` and `__call__`

options.label: B
options.text: `__len__` and `__getitem__` are required; `DataLoader` handles batching, shuffling, and parallel loading

options.label: C
options.text: `__iter__` and `__next__` only

options.label: D
options.text: `__getattr__` and `__setattr__`

explanation: `__len__` + `__getitem__` are required; DataLoader handles batching, shuffling, parallel loading.

title: Q79. What is transfer learning / fine-tuning? Show freezing layers.
description:
Q79. What is transfer learning / fine-tuning? Show freezing layers.

```python
for param in backbone.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False          # freeze pretrained layers
backbone.fc = nn.Linear(backbone.fc.in_features, n_classes)  # new head
```

options.label: A
options.text: Freeze the new classification head and train only the pretrained backbone

options.label: B
options.text: Freeze pretrained backbone layers (`requires_grad = False`), train only a new head first, then optionally unfreeze top layers with a small LR

options.label: C
options.text: Transfer learning requires training the entire network from scratch on the new task

options.label: D
options.text: Freezing layers has no effect on training speed or memory

explanation: Reuse representations learned on a large dataset; train only a new head first (feature extraction), then optionally unfreeze top layers with a small LR (fine-tuning). Saves data and compute.

title: Q80. Explain the attention mechanism (scaled dot-product).
description:
Q80. Explain the attention mechanism (scaled dot-product).

```python
def attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    scores = Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1) / math.sqrt(Q.size(-1))
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float("-inf"))
    return torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1) @ V
```

options.label: A
options.text: Scores = `Q @ K.T` without any scaling, then softmax and multiply by V

options.label: B
options.text: Scores = `(Q @ K.T) / sqrt(d_k)`, softmax over the scores, then a weighted sum of V — scaling by √d_k prevents softmax saturation

options.label: C
options.text: Attention uses only element-wise multiplication of Q and K, no matrix multiply

options.label: D
options.text: V is used to compute the scores; Q and K are only used for the final output

explanation:
Each query attends to all keys; softmax over similarity scores weights the values. Scaling by √d_k prevents softmax saturation. Multi-head attention runs this in parallel subspaces. This is the core of the Transformer.

---

## PART 7: GENAI / LLM ENGINEERING (Q81–Q100)

title: Q81. Explain the Transformer architecture at a high level.
description: Q81. Explain the Transformer architecture at a high level.

options.label: A
options.text: Sequential RNN cells with no attention mechanism

options.label: B
options.text: Stacked blocks of multi-head self-attention + feed-forward layers with residual connections and LayerNorm; decoder-only LLMs use causal masking so tokens see only left context; enables O(n²) attention with full parallelism during training

options.label: C
options.text: Transformers rely solely on convolutional layers for context

options.label: D
options.text: Attention is computed only once at the input layer, not per block

explanation: Stacked blocks of multi-head self-attention + position-wise feed-forward layers, each wrapped with residual connections and LayerNorm. Token + positional embeddings feed in; decoder-only LLMs (GPT-style) use causal (masked) attention so each token sees only its left context. Attention captures long-range dependencies in O(n²) with full parallelism during training (vs sequential RNNs).

title: Q82. What is tokenization? Why subword (BPE) instead of words?
description:
Q82. What is tokenization? Why subword (BPE) instead of words?

```python
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
tok.tokenize("unbelievable")   # ['un', 'believ', 'able']
```

options.label: A
options.text: Word-level tokenization is always preferred since it has no OOV problem

options.label: B
options.text: Subword methods (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece) balance vocabulary size vs sequence length and handle rare/unseen words by decomposition, avoiding OOV tokens entirely

options.label: C
options.text: Character-level tokenization is what modern LLMs use since it needs no learning

options.label: D
options.text: Tokenization is only used at inference time, not during training

explanation: Text → integer IDs the model consumes. Subword methods (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece) balance vocabulary size vs sequence length, handle rare/unseen words by decomposition, and avoid OOV tokens entirely.

title: Q83. Temperature, top-k, and top-p in LLM sampling?
description: Q83. Temperature, top-k, and top-p in LLM sampling?

options.label: A
options.text: Higher temperature always makes output more deterministic

options.label: B
options.text: Temperature scales logits before softmax (low T = near-greedy, high T = more random); top-k restricts sampling to the k most likely tokens; top-p (nucleus) samples from the smallest set whose cumulative probability ≥ p

options.label: C
options.text: Top-k and top-p do the same thing and are interchangeable in every implementation

options.label: D
options.text: Temperature is applied after top-p filtering, not before

explanation: Temperature scales logits before softmax: T→0 = near-greedy/deterministic, T>1 = flatter, more random. Top-k restricts sampling to the k most likely tokens. Top-p (nucleus) samples from the smallest set whose cumulative probability ≥ p — adapts set size to the distribution. Use low T for factual/extraction tasks, higher for creative generation.

title: Q84. Write code to call an LLM API with retry and timeout handling.

options.label: A
options.text: Retry indefinitely with no timeout and no backoff, to guarantee eventual success

options.label: B
options.text: Use a timeout per request, retry with exponential backoff on transient errors/rate limits, and re-raise after the final failed attempt

options.label: C
options.text: Never retry LLM API calls since failures are always permanent

options.label: D
options.text: Retry immediately without delay to minimize latency

explanation: Production LLM calls need timeout, retry with backoff for rate limits/transient errors, and (bonus) logging of tokens/cost per call.

title: Q85. What is RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) and why use it?
description: Q85. What is RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) and why use it?

options.label: A
options.text: Fine-tune the model on every new document as it arrives

options.label: B
options.text: Chunk documents → embed → store in a vector DB → at query time embed the query, retrieve top-k similar chunks, inject them into the prompt as context — grounds answers, reduces hallucination, updates via re-indexing without retraining

options.label: C
options.text: RAG replaces the need for an LLM entirely with a lookup table

options.label: D
options.text: RAG requires no embedding model, only keyword search

explanation: Pipeline: chunk documents → embed → store in a vector DB → at query time, embed the query, retrieve top-k similar chunks, and inject them into the LLM prompt as context. Benefits vs fine-tuning: grounds answers in current/private data, reduces hallucination, updates by re-indexing (no retraining), provides citations. Core components: chunker, embedding model, vector store, retriever, generator.

title: Q86. Implement cosine similarity search over embeddings.
description:
Q86. Implement cosine similarity search over embeddings.

```python
import numpy as np
def top_k_similar(query_emb, doc_embs, k=5):
    q = query_emb / np.linalg.norm(query_emb)
    D = doc_embs / np.linalg.norm(doc_embs, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    sims = D @ q
    idx = np.argpartition(sims, -k)[-k:]
    return idx[np.argsort(sims[idx])[::-1]], sims
```

options.label: A
options.text: Compute raw dot products without normalizing vectors, then sort ascending

options.label: B
options.text: Normalize query and document embeddings, compute the dot product (= cosine similarity), and use `argpartition` for O(n) top-k; use ANN indexes (FAISS/HNSW) at scale

options.label: C
options.text: Cosine similarity requires Euclidean distance, not dot products

options.label: D
options.text: Top-k must always be computed with a full sort, never `argpartition`

explanation: Normalize both sides so dot product = cosine similarity; `argpartition` for O(n) top-k. At scale, use ANN indexes (FAISS/HNSW) instead of brute force.

title: Q87. Describe chunking strategies for RAG and their trade-offs.
description: Q87. Describe chunking strategies for RAG and their trade-offs.

options.label: A
options.text: Only fixed-size chunking exists; overlap and semantic boundaries are irrelevant

options.label: B
options.text: Fixed-size with overlap, sentence/paragraph-aware (recursive) splitting, semantic chunking (split where embedding similarity drops), and structural (headers/functions) chunking — small chunks are precise but lose context, large chunks add context but dilute relevance

options.label: C
options.text: Larger chunks are always strictly better regardless of token cost

options.label: D
options.text: Chunk size has no effect on retrieval quality or cost

explanation: (1) Fixed-size with overlap (e.g., 500 tokens, 50 overlap) — simple, may split ideas mid-sentence. (2) Sentence/paragraph-aware (recursive splitting) — respects natural boundaries. (3) Semantic chunking — split where embedding similarity drops. (4) Structural — by headers/sections for docs, functions for code. Small chunks = precise retrieval but lost context; large chunks = more context but diluted relevance and higher token cost. Overlap mitigates boundary loss.

title: Q88. What is hallucination? List concrete mitigation strategies.
description: Q88. What is hallucination? List concrete mitigation strategies.

options.label: A
options.text: Increase temperature to make the model more careful

options.label: B
options.text: RAG grounding with "answer only from context" instructions, lower temperature, citations, self-consistency/verification passes, structured outputs with validation, and abstention when info isn't available

options.label: C
options.text: Hallucination cannot be reduced, only detected after the fact

options.label: D
options.text: Removing the system prompt eliminates hallucination

explanation: Confident generation of false or unsupported content. Mitigations: RAG grounding with instruction to answer only from context; lower temperature; require citations to sources; self-consistency / verification passes; structured outputs with validation; abstention ("say 'I don't know' if not in context"); evals that measure faithfulness (e.g., grounding checks against retrieved passages); human review for high-stakes outputs.

title: Q89. Compare fine-tuning vs RAG vs prompt engineering — when to use each?
description: Q89. Compare fine-tuning vs RAG vs prompt engineering — when to use each?

options.label: A
options.text: Fine-tuning should always be tried first since it's cheapest and fastest to iterate

options.label: B
options.text: Prompt engineering first (cheap, for style/format); RAG when knowledge is private/large/changing (gives citations); fine-tuning for consistent style/format or domain behavior at scale — not for injecting fresh facts. Often combined

options.label: C
options.text: RAG and fine-tuning solve identical problems and are interchangeable

options.label: D
options.text: Prompt engineering cannot change model behavior, only RAG and fine-tuning can

explanation: Prompt engineering: first resort — cheap, instant iteration; for behavior/format/style. RAG: when answers depend on private, large, or frequently changing knowledge; gives citations. Fine-tuning: when you need consistent style/format at scale, domain-specific behavior, smaller/cheaper models matching a big model's task performance, or latency/cost reduction — not for injecting fresh facts. Often combined: fine-tune for format + RAG for knowledge.

title: Q90. What is LoRA and why is it efficient?
description: Q90. What is LoRA and why is it efficient?

options.label: A
options.text: LoRA retrains all model weights but at a lower learning rate

options.label: B
options.text: LoRA freezes pretrained weights and learns a low-rank update ΔW = BA, cutting trainable parameters to often <1% of the model, reducing memory and enabling tiny swappable adapters

options.label: C
options.text: LoRA compresses the model by removing entire layers

options.label: D
options.text: LoRA requires more GPU memory than full fine-tuning because it adds extra matrices

explanation: Low-Rank Adaptation freezes pretrained weights W and learns a low-rank update ΔW = BA (B: d×r, A: r×k with r ≪ d), so trainable parameters drop from d×k to r(d+k) — often <1% of the model. Cuts GPU memory drastically, adapters are tiny files that can be swapped per task, and merged into W at inference with zero latency cost. QLoRA adds 4-bit quantization of the frozen base for further memory savings.

title: Q91. Write a prompt template for structured JSON extraction.
description:
Q91. Write a prompt template for structured JSON extraction.

```python
PROMPT = """Extract the following fields from the resume text.
Respond with ONLY valid JSON, no markdown fences, matching this schema:
{{"name": str, "email": str, "years_experience": int, "skills": [str]}}

Resume:
{resume_text}"""

import json
def extract(client, text):
    raw = call_llm(client, PROMPT.format(resume_text=text))
    raw = raw.strip().removeprefix("```json").removesuffix("```").strip()
    return json.loads(raw)
```

options.label: A
options.text: Ask the model to "try to return JSON" with no schema, and parse with regex

options.label: B
options.text: Give an explicit schema, instruct "ONLY valid JSON, no markdown fences," strip defensive fences, then `json.loads` (or use native structured-output/tool-calling for a guaranteed schema)

options.label: C
options.text: JSON extraction cannot be reliably prompted; it always requires fine-tuning

options.label: D
options.text: Markdown code fences should always be left in the raw output before parsing

explanation: Explicit schema, "ONLY valid JSON" instruction, defensive fence-stripping, and `json.loads` with error handling (retry on parse failure). Bonus: use the API's native structured-output/tool-calling for guaranteed schema.

title: Q92. What are embeddings? How do you choose an embedding model?
description: Q92. What are embeddings? How do you choose an embedding model?

options.label: A
options.text: Embeddings are sparse one-hot vectors used only for keyword matching

options.label: B
options.text: Embeddings are dense vectors where semantic similarity ≈ geometric closeness; choose by benchmarking on your data (or MTEB), considering dimension, max input length, domain match, multilingual needs, and hosted vs self-hosted trade-offs

options.label: C
options.text: All embedding models produce identical vector spaces, so choice doesn't matter

options.label: D
options.text: Embedding dimension has no effect on storage or retrieval speed

explanation: Dense vectors where semantic similarity ≈ geometric closeness — the foundation of semantic search, RAG retrieval, clustering, dedup, and recommendation. Choosing: benchmark on your own retrieval data (or MTEB as a proxy), consider dimension (storage/speed vs quality), max input length, domain match (code vs legal vs general), multilingual needs, and hosted-API vs self-hosted cost/privacy trade-offs.

title: Q93. Explain context window limits and strategies to work within them.
description: Q93. Explain context window limits and strategies to work within them.

options.label: A
options.text: Context windows are unlimited for modern LLMs, so no strategy is needed

options.label: B
options.text: Use retrieval (RAG) instead of stuffing everything in, summarize/compress older turns, hierarchical map-reduce summarization for long docs, prompt caching, and place critical instructions at start/end (avoiding the "lost in the middle" effect)

options.label: C
options.text: Attention cost is constant regardless of sequence length, so context limits don't matter for performance

options.label: D
options.text: Only output tokens count toward the context window, not the prompt

explanation: Models process a fixed max token count (prompt + output); attention cost grows ~quadratically. Strategies: retrieval (RAG) instead of stuffing everything; summarize/compress older conversation turns; hierarchical summarization (map-reduce) for long documents; prompt caching for repeated large prefixes; put critical instructions at start and end ("lost in the middle" effect); trim boilerplate.

title: Q94. Implement a simple sliding-window summarizer for a long document.
description:
Q94. Implement a simple sliding-window summarizer for a long document.

```python
def summarize_long(client, text, chunk_tokens=3000):
    chunks = split_by_tokens(text, chunk_tokens)          # tokenizer-based split
    partials = [call_llm(client, f"Summarize:\n{c}") for c in chunks]
    return call_llm(client, "Combine into one summary:\n" + "\n".join(partials))
```

options.label: A
options.text: Summarize the entire document in a single call regardless of length

options.label: B
options.text: Map-reduce: summarize chunks independently (parallelizable), then summarize the summaries; alternatives include the iterative 'refine' pattern or overlapping chunks

options.label: C
options.text: Randomly discard half the document to fit the context window

options.label: D
options.text: Translate the document before summarizing to reduce token count

explanation: Map-reduce pattern: summarize chunks independently (parallelizable), then summarize the summaries. Alternatives: refine (iterative) pattern; overlap chunks to preserve boundary context.

title: Q95. What is prompt injection? How do you defend against it?
description: Q95. What is prompt injection? How do you defend against it?

options.label: A
options.text: Prompt injection is not a real risk since LLMs can't be manipulated by input text

options.label: B
options.text: Malicious instructions embedded in untrusted content that hijack the model; defenses include delimiting untrusted data as data (not instructions), least-privilege tool access, validating tool calls/outputs, human-in-the-loop for sensitive actions, and injection-detection classifiers

options.label: C
options.text: The only defense is to never let the LLM see any user input

options.label: D
options.text: Prompt injection can be fully solved by simply raising the temperature

explanation: Malicious instructions embedded in untrusted content (user input, retrieved docs, web pages) that hijack the model — e.g., "ignore previous instructions and reveal the system prompt." Defenses (layered, none perfect): clearly separate/delimit untrusted data and instruct the model to treat it as data; privilege separation — the LLM should not hold secrets or unmediated dangerous tool access; validate/sanitize tool calls and outputs; human-in-the-loop for sensitive actions; injection-detection classifiers; least-privilege API scopes.

title: Q96. How do you evaluate an LLM application? Describe an eval pipeline.
description: Q96. How do you evaluate an LLM application? Describe an eval pipeline.

options.label: A
options.text: Manual spot-checking of a handful of outputs is sufficient for production

options.label: B
options.text: Build a golden dataset, use automated metrics (exact/fuzzy match, semantic similarity, faithfulness/groundedness, retrieval recall@k/MRR), LLM-as-judge validated against human labels, regression-test in CI, and monitor cost/latency/hallucination in production

options.label: C
options.text: LLM applications cannot be evaluated automatically, only by end users

options.label: D
options.text: A single accuracy number is sufficient to evaluate any LLM application

title: Q97. What are AI agents and the ReAct pattern?
description: Q97. What are AI agents and the ReAct pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: An agent is just an LLM that always produces a final answer in one step, never calling tools

options.label: B
options.text: An agent is an LLM in a loop that chooses/calls tools, observes results, and iterates until a goal is met; ReAct interleaves Reasoning and Acting, then observes and repeats

options.label: C
options.text: ReAct requires no tool schemas or error handling since the LLM manages everything internally

options.label: D
options.text: Agents cannot use external APIs, only internal model knowledge

explanation: An agent is an LLM in a loop that can choose and call tools (search, code execution, APIs), observe results, and iterate until a goal is met. ReAct interleaves Reasoning ("I need the user's orders first") and Acting (call `get_orders`), then observes and repeats. Key engineering concerns: tool schemas, loop/step limits, error handling and recovery, state/memory management, guarding side-effecting actions with confirmation.

title: Q98. Sketch the minimal agent loop with tool calling.
description:
Q98. Sketch the minimal agent loop with tool calling.

```python
def agent_loop(client, tools, messages, max_steps=10):
    for _ in range(max_steps):
        resp = client.messages.create(model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
                                      max_tokens=1024,
                                      tools=tools, messages=messages)
        if resp.stop_reason != "tool_use":
            return resp.content[0].text                 # final answer
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content})
        results = [ {"type": "tool_result",
                     "tool_use_id": b.id,
                     "content": run_tool(b.name, b.input)}
                    for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use"]
        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": results})
    return "Step limit reached"
```

options.label: A
options.text: Call the model once, always execute all available tools regardless of the response, then stop

options.label: B
options.text: Loop: call the model; if it requested tools, execute them and append results to messages, then continue; otherwise return the final answer; a step limit prevents infinite loops

options.label: C
options.text: Tools are called before the model responds, not after

options.label: D
options.text: The loop never checks `stop_reason`; it always assumes the model wants to use a tool

explanation: Loop: model responds → if it requested tools, execute them, append results, continue; else return the answer. `max_steps` prevents infinite loops.

title: Q99. RLHF vs supervised fine-tuning — what problem does RLHF solve?
description: Q99. RLHF vs supervised fine-tuning — what problem does RLHF solve?

options.label: A
options.text: SFT alone can rank "which of two plausible answers is better" just as well as RLHF

options.label: B
options.text: SFT imitates demonstration outputs but can't capture preference between plausible answers; RLHF trains a reward model on human preference rankings and optimizes the LLM against it (PPO) with KL regularization to the SFT model; DPO is a simpler alternative without an explicit reward model

options.label: C
options.text: RLHF replaces supervised fine-tuning entirely and is always done first

options.label: D
options.text: RLHF requires no human involvement, only automated reward signals

explanation: SFT teaches the model to imitate demonstration outputs, but demonstrations can't cover "which of two plausible answers is better." RLHF: collect human preference rankings over model outputs → train a reward model to predict preferences → optimize the LLM against it with RL (PPO), regularized by KL-divergence to the SFT model to prevent reward hacking/drift. DPO is a popular simpler alternative that optimizes preferences directly without an explicit reward model or RL loop. This alignment step is what makes models helpful, instruction-following, and safer.

title: Q100. Design a production RAG system end-to-end (system design question).
description: Q100. Design a production RAG system end-to-end (system design question).

options.label: A
options.text: A single vector search call directly returning the top-1 chunk to the user with no generation step

options.label: B
options.text: Ingestion (chunk/embed/index) → hybrid retrieval (vector + BM25) with reranking → generation with grounded, cited prompting → serving (caching, streaming, retries) → quality & safety (eval suite, injection defenses, monitoring)

options.label: C
options.text: Production RAG only needs a vector database; no reranking, caching, or evaluation is necessary

options.label: D
options.text: Chunking strategy and reranking have no measurable effect on RAG quality

explanation:
Expected architecture discussion:
1. **Ingestion:** document loaders → cleaning → structural/semantic chunking with overlap → embedding model → vector DB (FAISS/pgvector/Pinecone) + metadata store; incremental re-indexing on updates.
2. **Retrieval:** query embedding → hybrid search (vector + BM25 keyword) → metadata filtering → reranker (cross-encoder) on top-50 → top-5 to the prompt.
3. **Generation:** prompt with delimited context, "answer only from context, cite sources" instruction, low temperature, structured citations.
4. **Serving:** caching (embedding + response), streaming output, timeouts/retries, rate limits, cost tracking per request.
5. **Quality & safety:** eval suite (retrieval recall@k, faithfulness, LLM-as-judge), user feedback loop, prompt-injection defenses, PII handling, monitoring (latency, cost, hallucination rate). Senior signal: discusses trade-offs (hybrid vs pure vector, reranker latency vs quality, chunk size) and failure modes.

title: Q102. Your team is building a customer support assistant that must answer questions strictly from a 40-page internal policy PDF, with zero tolerance for the model inventing policy details. The PDF fits well within the context window of your chosen LLM. What is the most appropriate approach?
description: Q102. Your team is building a customer support assistant that must answer questions strictly from a 40-page internal policy PDF, with zero tolerance for the model inventing policy details. The PDF fits well within the context window of your chosen LLM. What is the most appropriate approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Fine-tune the LLM on the PDF content so it memorizes policy details

options.label: B
options.text: Place the full PDF content directly in the prompt context on every query (long-context prompting) with strict instructions to only answer from the provided text

options.label: C
options.text: Use a RAG pipeline with multiple retrieval steps even though the whole document already fits in context

options.label: D
options.text: Increase the model's temperature to 0 and rely on its general training knowledge of company policies

explanation: When the entire knowledge base reliably fits within context, direct long-context prompting with grounding instructions is simpler, cheaper to maintain, and easier to audit than fine-tuning (which risks stale knowledge and hallucination on edge cases) or RAG (unnecessary engineering overhead for a static, small corpus).

title: Q103. A product team wants an LLM-powered feature that classifies inbound support tickets into one of 12 fixed categories, at a volume of 2 million tickets per month, with a strict latency budget of under 300ms per ticket and a tight cost ceiling. Which approach best fits these constraints?
description: Q103. A product team wants an LLM-powered feature that classifies inbound support tickets into one of 12 fixed categories, at a volume of 2 million tickets per month, with a strict latency budget of under 300ms per ticket and a tight cost ceiling. Which approach best fits these constraints?

options.label: A
options.text: Call a large frontier LLM (e.g., GPT-4-class) via API for every ticket with a detailed classification prompt

options.label: B
options.text: Fine-tune or use a small, distilled classification model (or a lightweight encoder-based classifier) trained on labeled ticket data

options.label: C
options.text: Use a large LLM with chain-of-thought reasoning to justify each classification before outputting the label

options.label: D
options.text: Build a RAG pipeline that retrieves similar past tickets before each classification

explanation: At 2M/month with sub-300ms latency and tight cost constraints, a small fine-tuned or distilled classifier is far cheaper and faster than invoking a large LLM per call. Large-LLM approaches and RAG add unnecessary latency and cost for a bounded, well-defined classification task that doesn't require generative reasoning.

title: Q104. You're prompting an LLM to extract structured fields (policy number, claim amount, date of loss) from free-text insurance claim notes, and feeding the output directly into a downstream database without human review. Which prompting strategy most reduces the risk of malformed or unparseable output reaching the database?
description: Q104. You're prompting an LLM to extract structured fields (policy number, claim amount, date of loss) from free-text insurance claim notes, and feeding the output directly into a downstream database without human review. Which prompting strategy most reduces the risk of malformed or unparseable output reaching the database?

options.label: A
options.text: Ask the model conversationally to 'list out the key fields it can find in the text'

options.label: B
options.text: Use a few-shot prompt with example input/output pairs, combined with a strict JSON schema and structured output / function-calling mode supported by the LLM provider

options.label: C
options.text: Increase the temperature to encourage the model to be more thorough in finding fields

options.label: D
options.text: Ask the model to extract the fields and then separately ask it to double check its own answer in the same call

explanation: Structured output / function-calling modes enforce schema conformance at the API level, and few-shot examples anchor the model's format expectations — this combination is the most reliable way to guarantee machine-parseable output. Free-form conversational prompting and self-checking within a single call do not guarantee schema validity.

title: Q105. Your team has a GenAI application in production where the same prompt occasionally produces inconsistent JSON key names across calls (e.g., 'claim_amount' vs 'claimAmount'), breaking downstream parsing intermittently. The team has already adopted a JSON schema in their structured-output configuration. What is the most likely root cause and fix?
description: Q105. Your team has a GenAI application in production where the same prompt occasionally produces inconsistent JSON key names across calls (e.g., 'claim_amount' vs 'claimAmount'), breaking downstream parsing intermittently. The team has already adopted a JSON schema in their structured-output configuration. What is the most likely root cause and fix?

options.label: A
options.text: The model is fundamentally incapable of consistent output and should be replaced with a rules-based parser

options.label: B
options.text: The structured output schema is likely not being strictly enforced (e.g., using a 'best-effort' JSON mode rather than a strict schema-validated mode), and the fix is to switch to a strict schema-constrained decoding mode if the provider supports it

options.label: C
options.text: The issue is caused by network latency and can be fixed by adding retries

options.label: D
options.text: The team should lower the temperature to 0 only, since structured output schemas are advisory and temperature is the only real lever for consistency

explanation: Many providers offer both a lenient 'JSON mode' (best-effort, no guaranteed schema match) and a stricter schema-constrained / function-calling mode that enforces exact key names via grammar-constrained decoding. If the team is using the lenient mode, drift is expected; the fix is to use the strict mode, not just adjust temperature.

title: Q106. Your company wants to deploy a GenAI assistant on sensitive financial data and is choosing between an API-hosted frontier model and a self-hosted open-weight model. Data residency and the inability to send raw customer financial records to any third-party endpoint are non-negotiable, hard requirements from legal. Cost and quality are secondary considerations. What should drive the decision?
description: Q106. Your company wants to deploy a GenAI assistant on sensitive financial data and is choosing between an API-hosted frontier model and a self-hosted open-weight model. Data residency and the inability to send raw customer financial records to any third-party endpoint are non-negotiable, hard requirements from legal. Cost and quality are secondary considerations. What should drive the decision?

options.label: A
options.text: Choose the API-hosted frontier model since it will always have the highest accuracy regardless of constraints

options.label: B
options.text: Choose a self-hosted open-weight model deployed within the company's own infrastructure or VPC, since the hard data residency constraint eliminates third-party API options regardless of quality differences

options.label: C
options.text: Use the API-hosted model but strip personally identifiable information before sending, treating this as equivalent to full data residency compliance

options.label: D
options.text: Negotiate a zero-data-retention agreement with the API provider and treat that as sufficient to satisfy the data residency requirement

explanation: When data residency is a hard, non-negotiable legal constraint (not a risk-tolerance question), the data must never leave the company's infrastructure — which rules out third-party APIs entirely, regardless of zero-retention promises or PII stripping (which is often imperfect and doesn't address residency/jurisdiction). Self-hosting is the only architecture that satisfies a true hard requirement.

title: Q107. A team observes that their LLM-based summarization feature works well on short documents but produces summaries that ignore content from the middle of longer documents, even though the documents fit within the model's stated context window. What phenomenon most likely explains this?
description: Q107. A team observes that their LLM-based summarization feature works well on short documents but produces summaries that ignore content from the middle of longer documents, even though the documents fit within the model's stated context window. What phenomenon most likely explains this?

options.label: A
options.text: The model's context window is being silently truncated by the API provider

options.label: B
options.text: 'Lost in the middle' — a known behavior where LLMs attend less reliably to information in the middle of long contexts compared to the beginning and end

options.label: C
options.text: The tokenizer is failing to encode the middle portion of the document

options.label: D
options.text: The summarization prompt is too short to instruct the model properly

explanation: This is a well-documented LLM behavior often called 'lost in the middle,' where models show stronger recall for content near the start and end of a long context than content buried in the middle, even when technically within the context window limit.

title: Q108. Your team is comparing two LLM vendors for a customer-facing chatbot. Vendor A's model has a lower benchmark score on general reasoning leaderboards but was fine-tuned on data closely resembling your domain (insurance claims dialogue). Vendor B's model tops general leaderboards but has no domain exposure. Internal testing on your actual use case shows Vendor A's model outperforms Vendor B's on your specific evaluation set. Which decision is best supported by this evidence?
description: Q108. Your team is comparing two LLM vendors for a customer-facing chatbot. Vendor A's model has a lower benchmark score on general reasoning leaderboards but was fine-tuned on data closely resembling your domain (insurance claims dialogue). Vendor B's model tops general leaderboards but has no domain exposure. Internal testing on your actual use case shows Vendor A's model outperforms Vendor B's on your specific evaluation set. Which decision is best supported by this evidence?

options.label: A
options.text: Choose Vendor B because general leaderboard performance is the most reliable predictor of production performance

options.label: B
options.text: Choose Vendor A, because task-specific evaluation on your own representative data is a stronger signal for your use case than general-purpose leaderboard rankings

options.label: C
options.text: Choose whichever vendor offers the lower API price, since both models are 'good enough' according to leaderboards

options.label: D
options.text: Re-run the general leaderboard benchmarks yourself to validate Vendor B's claimed scores before deciding

explanation: General benchmarks measure broad capability but are poor proxies for domain-specific performance. A model evaluated directly on representative, in-domain data is the more reliable signal for a production decision — this is a core GenAI engineering principle: build your own eval set before trusting public leaderboards.

title: Q109. An engineer notices their LLM application's cost has grown significantly even though traffic volume hasn't increased. Investigation shows the conversation history (including all prior turns) is being resent in full on every new turn of a multi-turn chat. What is the most effective way to control this cost growth while preserving conversational coherence?
description: Q109. An engineer notices their LLM application's cost has grown significantly even though traffic volume hasn't increased. Investigation shows the conversation history (including all prior turns) is being resent in full on every new turn of a multi-turn chat. What is the most effective way to control this cost growth while preserving conversational coherence?

options.label: A
options.text: Switch to a cheaper model with the same context-resending pattern, since the architecture itself is not the issue

options.label: B
options.text: Implement a context management strategy such as conversation summarization, sliding window truncation, or selective memory retrieval, so older turns are compressed or pruned rather than resent verbatim

options.label: C
options.text: Cap the number of conversation turns a user is allowed to have, regardless of UX impact

options.label: D
options.text: Disable multi-turn context entirely and treat every message as a fresh, stateless request

explanation: Resending full history is a classic cost-scaling problem (cost grows roughly quadratically with conversation length). The standard fix is context management — summarizing or windowing older turns — which controls cost while preserving the experience, unlike disabling memory entirely or arbitrarily capping turns.

title: Q110. You are designing a prompt for an LLM to triage incoming insurance claims into 'urgent,' 'standard,' or 'low-priority.' Initial testing shows the model is inconsistent: identical claims sometimes get different labels across reruns. The team wants more deterministic behavior without retraining. What is the most direct lever to address this?
description: Q110. You are designing a prompt for an LLM to triage incoming insurance claims into 'urgent,' 'standard,' or 'low-priority.' Initial testing shows the model is inconsistent: identical claims sometimes get different labels across reruns. The team wants more deterministic behavior without retraining. What is the most direct lever to address this?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase the temperature parameter to add more variety and 'average out' inconsistency over time

options.label: B
options.text: Lower the temperature toward 0 (and/or set a fixed random seed if supported) to reduce sampling randomness, while keeping the prompt and few-shot examples otherwise unchanged

options.label: C
options.text: Switch to a larger model, since inconsistency is purely a function of model size

options.label: D
options.text: Remove the few-shot examples, since examples are introducing the inconsistency

explanation: Temperature directly controls sampling randomness; lowering it toward 0 makes the model's outputs far more deterministic for classification-style tasks. Model size and few-shot examples are not the primary lever for output-to-output consistency — temperature (and seed, where supported) is.

title: Q111. A team is deciding between prompt engineering, RAG, and fine-tuning to teach an LLM-based assistant a company's evolving product catalog, which changes weekly with new SKUs and pricing. Which approach is best suited to this scenario, and why?
description: Q111. A team is deciding between prompt engineering, RAG, and fine-tuning to teach an LLM-based assistant a company's evolving product catalog, which changes weekly with new SKUs and pricing. Which approach is best suited to this scenario, and why?

options.label: A
options.text: Fine-tuning, because it permanently bakes the knowledge into model weights for fast inference

options.label: B
options.text: RAG, because it allows the catalog data to be updated independently of the model by simply updating the retrieval index, avoiding the cost and lag of retraining for weekly changes

options.label: C
options.text: Prompt engineering alone, by asking the model to 'always use the latest product information' without providing any external data

options.label: D
options.text: Fine-tuning combined with weekly full retraining cycles to keep the model current

explanation: For frequently changing, factual knowledge, RAG is the standard choice because the underlying data store can be updated instantly without retraining. Fine-tuning is poorly suited to fast-changing facts (retraining weekly is costly and operationally fragile), and prompt engineering alone cannot supply information the model was never given.

title: Q112. Your team wants to reduce LLM API costs for a high-volume internal tool without significantly degrading output quality. Profiling shows that most of the token cost comes from a long, static system prompt containing detailed formatting instructions and five few-shot examples, repeated on every single call. What is the most effective first optimization to try?
description: Q112. Your team wants to reduce LLM API costs for a high-volume internal tool without significantly degrading output quality. Profiling shows that most of the token cost comes from a long, static system prompt containing detailed formatting instructions and five few-shot examples, repeated on every single call. What is the most effective first optimization to try?

options.label: A
options.text: Switch to a smaller model immediately, without examining the prompt structure

options.label: B
options.text: Use prompt caching (if supported by the provider) for the static system prompt and few-shot portion, so that repeated, unchanged content is not re-billed at full price on every call

options.label: C
options.text: Remove all few-shot examples to shorten the prompt, regardless of quality impact

options.label: D
options.text: Ask users to write shorter queries to reduce token usage

explanation: When a large portion of the prompt is static and repeated verbatim across calls, provider-side prompt caching is the most direct cost lever — it preserves quality (the static instructions and examples are untouched) while substantially cutting cost, unlike blindly downsizing the model or stripping examples that may be load-bearing for quality.

title: Q113. A legal team asks your GenAI assistant to summarize contracts and flag risky clauses. During a review, you discover the model occasionally fabricates a clause number or misattributes a clause to the wrong section, while the surrounding summary text reads fluently and confidently. What is this phenomenon most accurately called, and what is the most appropriate mitigation given the high-stakes legal context?
description: Q113. A legal team asks your GenAI assistant to summarize contracts and flag risky clauses. During a review, you discover the model occasionally fabricates a clause number or misattributes a clause to the wrong section, while the surrounding summary text reads fluently and confidently. What is this phenomenon most accurately called, and what is the most appropriate mitigation given the high-stakes legal context?

options.label: A
options.text: This is a 'tokenizer bug,' and the fix is to use a different tokenizer

options.label: B
options.text: This is 'hallucination'; the most appropriate mitigation is to require the model to cite verbatim source spans for each flagged clause (grounding/citation), paired with a human review step before any output is acted upon

options.label: C
options.text: This is expected model behavior in any GenAI system and does not require mitigation since the summary is 'mostly correct'

options.label: D
options.text: This is caused by an overly high context window and can be fixed by truncating the contract before summarization

explanation: Confident but fabricated details are a textbook hallucination. In high-stakes domains like legal review, the standard mitigation is grounding outputs in verifiable source citations plus mandatory human-in-the-loop review — not dismissing it as acceptable or misdiagnosing it as a tooling bug.

title: Q114. Which of the following best describes the difference between zero-shot and few-shot prompting?
description: Q114. Which of the following best describes the difference between zero-shot and few-shot prompting?

options.label: A
options.text: Zero-shot prompting always produces worse results than few-shot prompting on every task

options.label: B
options.text: Zero-shot prompting gives the model a task with no examples, while few-shot prompting includes a small number of example input-output pairs to demonstrate the desired pattern

options.label: C
options.text: Zero-shot prompting can only be used with fine-tuned models, while few-shot requires a base model

options.label: D
options.text: Few-shot prompting requires retraining the model on the provided examples before inference

explanation: Zero-shot means no examples are given (just instructions), while few-shot provides a handful of demonstration examples in-context to steer output format and style — this is an in-context learning technique, not a retraining step.

title: Q115. A team building a code-generation assistant notices that increasing the maximum output token limit doesn't fix cases where the model's response is cut off mid-sentence. Investigation shows the model is actually hitting the combined input+output context window limit, not the output token cap. What is the most appropriate fix?
description: Q115. A team building a code-generation assistant notices that increasing the maximum output token limit doesn't fix cases where the model's response is cut off mid-sentence. Investigation shows the model is actually hitting the combined input+output context window limit, not the output token cap. What is the most appropriate fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep increasing the output token limit indefinitely until the issue disappears

options.label: B
options.text: Reduce the size of the input (e.g., trim unnecessary context, retrieved chunks, or conversation history) so that input + expected output together fit within the model's total context window, or switch to a model with a larger context window if the use case genuinely requires more

options.label: C
options.text: Switch the model's temperature to 0, since cut-off responses are caused by high randomness

options.label: D
options.text: Ignore the issue since context window limits cannot be worked around

explanation: Most LLMs share a single context budget across input and output tokens. If responses are truncated due to the combined limit, raising the output cap alone won't help — the fix is reducing input size or moving to a model with more total context, addressing the actual bottleneck.

title: Q116. Your team is evaluating whether to use a single large general-purpose LLM for an entire complex workflow (document parsing, classification, summarization, and report generation) versus chaining together smaller specialized models or prompts for each step. The workflow has well-defined, separable sub-tasks. What is the strongest argument for the chained, multi-step approach in this case?
description: Q116. Your team is evaluating whether to use a single large general-purpose LLM for an entire complex workflow (document parsing, classification, summarization, and report generation) versus chaining together smaller specialized models or prompts for each step. The workflow has well-defined, separable sub-tasks. What is the strongest argument for the chained, multi-step approach in this case?

options.label: A
options.text: A single large model will always be cheaper than running multiple smaller steps

options.label: B
options.text: Breaking the workflow into separable steps allows each step to be independently evaluated, debugged, and optimized (including swapping in cheaper/specialized models per step), improving reliability and making failures easier to localize, at the cost of added orchestration complexity

options.label: C
options.text: Multi-step chains eliminate the possibility of hallucination entirely

options.label: D
options.text: A single model approach is always preferable because it minimizes total latency

explanation: When sub-tasks are well-separated, decomposing the workflow makes each step independently testable, debuggable, and tunable (e.g., a cheap classifier for triage, a stronger model for generation) — improving reliability and maintainability, though it does add orchestration overhead and isn't a guarantee against hallucination.

title: Q117. Your team is choosing an embedding model to power semantic search over internal documents primarily written in a mix of English and regional Indian languages. Which factor should weigh most heavily in the embedding model selection?
description: Q117. Your team is choosing an embedding model to power semantic search over internal documents primarily written in a mix of English and regional Indian languages. Which factor should weigh most heavily in the embedding model selection?

options.label: A
options.text: Choosing the embedding model with the highest dimensionality, since higher dimensions always yield better semantic search quality

options.label: B
options.text: Verifying the embedding model's training data and benchmark performance explicitly cover multilingual content relevant to the document mix, since embedding quality degrades significantly for languages underrepresented in training

options.label: C
options.text: Choosing the cheapest available embedding API regardless of language coverage, since all embedding models perform equivalently across languages

options.label: D
options.text: Choosing an embedding model trained only on code, since code embeddings generalize well to all text types

explanation: Embedding quality is highly sensitive to the languages represented in training data. For a multilingual corpus, verifying explicit multilingual support and benchmarks is the deciding factor — dimensionality alone doesn't guarantee semantic quality across underrepresented languages.

title: Q118. A GenAI feature in production occasionally returns responses in a different language than the user's query, despite the system prompt instructing 'always respond in the same language as the user.' This happens more often with short, ambiguous queries (e.g., single words or abbreviations). What is the most likely cause and best mitigation?
description: Q118. A GenAI feature in production occasionally returns responses in a different language than the user's query, despite the system prompt instructing 'always respond in the same language as the user.' This happens more often with short, ambiguous queries (e.g., single words or abbreviations). What is the most likely cause and best mitigation?

options.label: A
options.text: The model is broken and should be replaced entirely

options.label: B
options.text: Short, ambiguous inputs give the model insufficient signal to reliably detect language; a more robust mitigation is to explicitly detect the input language programmatically (using a language-ID step) and pass it explicitly to the model, rather than relying on the model to infer it from a short query

options.label: C
options.text: The system prompt instruction should be repeated five times for emphasis

options.label: D
options.text: Increasing the temperature will fix language detection accuracy

explanation: Short or ambiguous text provides weak signal for implicit language detection by the model itself. The more reliable engineering fix is to detect language explicitly with a dedicated step and pass it as an explicit parameter, removing the ambiguity rather than hoping the model infers correctly from minimal context.

title: Q119. Your company is deciding whether to adopt a 'prompt chaining' pattern (sequential LLM calls, each step's output feeding the next) or a single mega-prompt that tries to accomplish a multi-step reasoning task in one call. Testing shows the mega-prompt occasionally skips intermediate steps under load, while prompt chaining is more reliable but has higher latency and cost due to multiple round trips. For a workflow where correctness matters more than speed (e.g., financial risk scoring), what is the most defensible recommendation?
description: Q119. Your company is deciding whether to adopt a 'prompt chaining' pattern (sequential LLM calls, each step's output feeding the next) or a single mega-prompt that tries to accomplish a multi-step reasoning task in one call. Testing shows the mega-prompt occasionally skips intermediate steps under load, while prompt chaining is more reliable but has higher latency and cost due to multiple round trips. For a workflow where correctness matters more than speed (e.g., financial risk scoring), what is the most defensible recommendation?

options.label: A
options.text: Always choose the mega-prompt approach since fewer API calls is inherently better practice

options.label: B
options.text: Favor prompt chaining despite the latency/cost tradeoff, since the task explicitly prioritizes correctness, and the mega-prompt has demonstrated reliability problems under load that directly threaten that priority

options.label: C
options.text: Randomly alternate between both approaches to average out the risk

options.label: D
options.text: Avoid LLMs entirely for this task and replace it with a fixed rules engine

explanation: When the stated priority is correctness over speed, and there's direct evidence the single mega-prompt skips steps under load, the decision should follow the evidence and the stated priority — chaining trades cost/latency for reliability, which is the right tradeoff given the task's risk profile.

title: Q120. What is the primary purpose of a 'system prompt' in an LLM API call, as distinct from the 'user prompt'?
description: Q120. What is the primary purpose of a 'system prompt' in an LLM API call, as distinct from the 'user prompt'?

options.label: A
options.text: The system prompt is just a longer version of the user prompt with no functional difference

options.label: B
options.text: The system prompt sets persistent behavior, role, tone, and constraints for the model across the conversation, while the user prompt carries the specific request or query for that turn

options.label: C
options.text: The system prompt is only used for fine-tuning and has no effect during inference

options.label: D
options.text: The system prompt is automatically generated by the model and cannot be customized by developers

explanation: The system prompt establishes the model's persona, behavioral guardrails, and task framing that should hold across the interaction, while the user prompt is the specific, turn-level input — a foundational distinction in prompt engineering.

title: Q121. A team building an LLM-powered email drafting assistant wants to prevent the model from ever generating content that includes specific banned phrases (e.g., promotional claims that violate compliance rules), with zero tolerance for any leakage. They currently rely solely on instructing the model via the system prompt to avoid these phrases. What is the most robust way to strengthen this guarantee?
description: Q121. A team building an LLM-powered email drafting assistant wants to prevent the model from ever generating content that includes specific banned phrases (e.g., promotional claims that violate compliance rules), with zero tolerance for any leakage. They currently rely solely on instructing the model via the system prompt to avoid these phrases. What is the most robust way to strengthen this guarantee?

options.label: A
options.text: Trust the system prompt instruction alone, since well-instructed LLMs never violate explicit constraints

options.label: B
options.text: Add a deterministic post-generation validation/filtering step (e.g., regex or keyword scan) that checks the model's output against the banned phrase list before it is shown to the user, since prompt instructions alone are probabilistic and not a hard guarantee

options.label: C
options.text: Increase the temperature so the model is more creative in avoiding the banned phrases

options.label: D
options.text: Switch to a larger model, since larger models never violate system prompt instructions

explanation: LLM instruction-following is probabilistic, not a hard guarantee — for zero-tolerance compliance requirements, a deterministic post-generation check (rules-based filter) is necessary as a backstop, regardless of how well the prompt is engineered or how large the model is.

title: Q122. Your team is benchmarking two candidate LLMs for a summarization task using ROUGE scores against reference summaries. Model A scores higher on ROUGE but human reviewers consistently rate Model B's summaries as more useful and accurate. What does this discrepancy most likely indicate?
description: Q122. Your team is benchmarking two candidate LLMs for a summarization task using ROUGE scores against reference summaries. Model A scores higher on ROUGE but human reviewers consistently rate Model B's summaries as more useful and accurate. What does this discrepancy most likely indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: The human reviewers are wrong and ROUGE should be trusted as the ground truth

options.label: B
options.text: ROUGE measures n-gram overlap with reference text and does not directly capture factual accuracy, coherence, or usefulness, so it can diverge from human judgment — human evaluation (or LLM-as-judge calibrated against humans) should be weighted more heavily for production decisions

options.label: C
options.text: ROUGE scores are randomly generated and have no meaning

options.label: D
options.text: The discrepancy means both models are equally good and the choice doesn't matter

explanation: ROUGE is a surface-level lexical overlap metric and is well known to correlate imperfectly with actual summary quality, faithfulness, or usefulness. When automated metrics and human judgment diverge, human evaluation is generally the more trustworthy signal for production decisions, especially for generative quality.

title: Q123. An LLM-powered chatbot occasionally responds to a completely unrelated follow-up question by continuing to discuss the topic from several turns earlier, ignoring the user's actual new question. The conversation history is being passed in full each turn. What is the most likely cause?
description: Q123. An LLM-powered chatbot occasionally responds to a completely unrelated follow-up question by continuing to discuss the topic from several turns earlier, ignoring the user's actual new question. The conversation history is being passed in full each turn. What is the most likely cause?

options.label: A
options.text: The model has a hardware malfunction

options.label: B
options.text: The model is attending strongly to earlier conversational context (recency/topic anchoring), and the new query may be too brief or ambiguous relative to the accumulated context, causing it to default toward continuing the prior topic instead of recognizing the topic shift

options.label: C
options.text: The API is caching old responses and serving them again

options.label: D
options.text: This indicates the model has run out of available tokens entirely

explanation: When a short, ambiguous follow-up is sent alongside a long prior context, the model can over-weight the established topic rather than detecting a topic shift. This is a context-management/prompt-design issue, not a hardware or caching fault, and is typically mitigated by explicit topic-shift handling or context pruning.

title: Q124. Your team wants to add a 'confidence score' to LLM-generated answers in a customer-facing application, so low-confidence answers can be routed to a human agent. Which approach is most reliable?
description: Q124. Your team wants to add a 'confidence score' to LLM-generated answers in a customer-facing application, so low-confidence answers can be routed to a human agent. Which approach is most reliable?

options.label: A
options.text: Ask the model to state a confidence percentage (e.g., '95% confident') directly in its own response text

options.label: B
options.text: Use a combination of model-external signals — such as retrieval relevance scores (if RAG is used), output consistency across repeated samples (self-consistency), or a separate calibrated classifier — rather than relying solely on the model's self-reported confidence, since LLMs are known to be poorly calibrated when asked to self-assess

options.label: C
options.text: Always treat every response as low confidence to be maximally safe

options.label: D
options.text: Use the model's temperature setting as a direct proxy for confidence

explanation: LLM self-reported confidence is well documented to be poorly calibrated — models often state high confidence even when wrong. More reliable signals come from external measures: retrieval relevance, self-consistency across samples, or a dedicated calibrated model, rather than trusting the model's own stated percentage.

title: Q125. A team is deciding between using an LLM with a very large context window to stuff in dozens of retrieved documents 'just in case,' versus a tighter retrieval pipeline that returns only the top 3-5 most relevant chunks. Testing shows the large-context approach has higher cost and slightly worse answer accuracy than the tighter retrieval approach, despite having access to more information. What best explains this counterintuitive result?
description: Q125. A team is deciding between using an LLM with a very large context window to stuff in dozens of retrieved documents 'just in case,' versus a tighter retrieval pipeline that returns only the top 3-5 most relevant chunks. Testing shows the large-context approach has higher cost and slightly worse answer accuracy than the tighter retrieval approach, despite having access to more information. What best explains this counterintuitive result?

options.label: A
options.text: Larger context windows always improve accuracy, so the test results must be measurement error

options.label: B
options.text: Irrelevant or low-relevance retrieved content acts as noise that can distract the model and dilute attention away from the most relevant chunks, so precision in retrieval (returning fewer, more relevant chunks) often outperforms simply maximizing recall by including everything

options.label: C
options.text: The large-context approach failed only because of a tokenizer bug specific to long contexts

options.label: D
options.text: Cost and accuracy are unrelated, so this is purely a coincidence with no underlying cause

explanation: This reflects a well-known RAG finding: dumping in many marginally relevant chunks can dilute the model's attention and introduce noise, hurting accuracy. A precision-focused retrieval strategy (fewer, higher-relevance chunks) frequently outperforms a recall-maximizing 'include everything' approach, even though it provides less raw information.

title: Q126. A new team member asks why the team uses 'temperature' and 'top-p' (nucleus sampling) parameters when calling an LLM API. What is the most accurate explanation?
description: Q126. A new team member asks why the team uses 'temperature' and 'top-p' (nucleus sampling) parameters when calling an LLM API. What is the most accurate explanation?

options.label: A
options.text: They control the physical processing speed and hardware temperature of the GPU running the model

options.label: B
options.text: They control the randomness/diversity of the model's token sampling during generation — lower values make output more deterministic and focused, higher values make output more varied and creative

options.label: C
options.text: They determine which fine-tuned version of the model is being called

options.label: D
options.text: They control how many tokens the model is allowed to generate in total

explanation: Temperature and top-p are sampling parameters that shape how the model selects the next token from its probability distribution — lower settings sharpen the distribution toward the most likely tokens (more deterministic), while higher settings flatten it (more diverse/creative). They have nothing to do with hardware or output token limits.

title: Q127. You are building a RAG system over a corpus of legal contracts where individual clauses often span 300-800 tokens and reference defined terms found elsewhere in the same contract. Naive fixed-size chunking at 200 tokens is splitting clauses mid-sentence and separating defined terms from their usage. What is the most appropriate fix?
description: Q127. You are building a RAG system over a corpus of legal contracts where individual clauses often span 300-800 tokens and reference defined terms found elsewhere in the same contract. Naive fixed-size chunking at 200 tokens is splitting clauses mid-sentence and separating defined terms from their usage. What is the most appropriate fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep the 200-token chunks but increase the number of retrieved chunks (top-k) to 50 to compensate

options.label: B
options.text: Increase the chunk size to 200,000 tokens so every document becomes a single chunk and splitting is no longer an issue

options.label: C
options.text: Avoid chunking and embed each contract as a single average vector across all its tokens

options.label: D
options.text: Switch to a structure-aware chunking strategy (e.g., splitting on clause/section boundaries using document structure or semantic similarity) with a larger chunk size and some overlap, so logical units stay intact

explanation: When content has natural logical boundaries (clauses, sections) that fixed-size chunking ignores, structure-aware chunking aligned to those boundaries (with modest overlap to preserve cross-references) directly fixes the root cause. Single-document-as-one-chunk and brute-force top-k inflation don't address the underlying splitting problem and introduce their own retrieval-precision issues.

title: Q128. Your RAG system retrieves the correct source document 95% of the time (measured via retrieval recall against a labeled eval set), but end-to-end answer accuracy is only 70%. Manual review shows the model is sometimes given the right document but still produces an incorrect or incomplete answer. Where should the team focus debugging effort first?
description: Q128. Your RAG system retrieves the correct source document 95% of the time (measured via retrieval recall against a labeled eval set), but end-to-end answer accuracy is only 70%. Manual review shows the model is sometimes given the right document but still produces an incorrect or incomplete answer. Where should the team focus debugging effort first?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase the embedding model's dimensionality to improve retrieval beyond 95%

options.label: B
options.text: Re-tune the retrieval pipeline further, since 95% recall is still not perfect

options.label: C
options.text: Switch to a different vector database, assuming the issue must be infrastructure-related

options.label: D
options.text: Investigate the generation step: prompt design, chunk granularity reaching the model, and whether the retrieved chunk actually contains the specific fact needed (not just topical relevance) - since retrieval is already performing well and the gap is downstream

explanation: With retrieval recall already high (95%) and the failure occurring after the right document is found, the bottleneck is downstream - either the chunk doesn't contain the precise fact despite being topically relevant, or the generation/prompt step is mishandling the retrieved context. Further retrieval tuning or infra changes wouldn't address a generation-side problem.

title: Q129. You're choosing between pure dense (embedding-based) retrieval and a hybrid approach combining dense retrieval with sparse keyword search (e.g., BM25) for a RAG system over technical support documentation that contains many exact product codes, error codes, and SKU numbers (e.g., 'ERR-4471', 'SKU-88210'). Users frequently search using these exact codes. Which approach is most appropriate?
description: Q129. You're choosing between pure dense (embedding-based) retrieval and a hybrid approach combining dense retrieval with sparse keyword search (e.g., BM25) for a RAG system over technical support documentation that contains many exact product codes, error codes, and SKU numbers (e.g., 'ERR-4471', 'SKU-88210'). Users frequently search using these exact codes. Which approach is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Pure sparse (keyword) retrieval only, setting aside dense retrieval, since all support queries involve exact codes

options.label: B
options.text: Neither - skip retrieval and have the model generate answers about error codes from its training knowledge alone

options.label: C
options.text: Pure dense retrieval, assuming embedding models reliably capture exact alphanumeric codes through semantic similarity

options.label: D
options.text: Hybrid retrieval combining dense and sparse (keyword) search, since exact codes and identifiers are often poorly represented in dense embedding space but are reliably matched by keyword/lexical search

explanation: Dense embeddings are excellent for semantic/conceptual similarity but notoriously weak at exact-match retrieval for identifiers, codes, and rare tokens, since these don't carry strong semantic meaning in embedding space. Hybrid retrieval (dense + sparse/BM25) is the standard fix, capturing both semantic queries and exact-code lookups - pure dense or pure sparse alone leaves a real gap.

title: Q130. Your RAG system's knowledge base contains both a 2023 policy document and a 2026 revised version, and the 2023 version was never removed from the index. Users occasionally receive answers based on outdated 2023 policy details. The team wants a fix without manually auditing the entire corpus by hand. What is the most scalable and reliable mitigation?
description: Q130. Your RAG system's knowledge base contains both a 2023 policy document and a 2026 revised version, and the 2023 version was never removed from the index. Users occasionally receive answers based on outdated 2023 policy details. The team wants a fix without manually auditing the entire corpus by hand. What is the most scalable and reliable mitigation?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase the number of retrieved chunks so the model sees both versions and can compare them itself

options.label: B
options.text: Ask the model in the prompt to 'prefer newer information when possible' and trust it to infer document recency from content alone

options.label: C
options.text: Implement a deterministic document lifecycle process: tag documents with effective dates/versions in metadata, filter or deprioritize superseded versions at retrieval time using that metadata, and remove or archive outdated documents from the active index

options.label: D
options.text: Switch to a larger LLM, since larger models are better at resolving conflicting source documents

explanation: Relying on the model to infer recency from unstructured content is unreliable and doesn't scale. The robust fix is treating document freshness as a first-class metadata/index-management problem - explicit versioning, metadata-based filtering at retrieval time, and removing superseded content from the active index - rather than hoping prompt instructions or a bigger model compensate for stale data in the source of truth.

title: Q131. A team is building a RAG system and debating whether to add a reranking step (a separate model that re-scores the initial top-50 retrieved chunks down to a final top-5 before passing to the LLM) versus relying solely on the vector store's similarity ranking. Initial retrieval uses a fast, lightweight embedding model optimized for speed over precision. What is the strongest argument for adding a reranker in this setup?
description: Q131. A team is building a RAG system and debating whether to add a reranking step (a separate model that re-scores the initial top-50 retrieved chunks down to a final top-5 before passing to the LLM) versus relying solely on the vector store's similarity ranking. Initial retrieval uses a fast, lightweight embedding model optimized for speed over precision. What is the strongest argument for adding a reranker in this setup?

options.label: A
options.text: A reranker, typically a more computationally expensive but more accurate cross-encoder model, can correct ordering mistakes from a fast-but-approximate initial retrieval pass, improving final precision without needing to run the expensive model over the entire corpus

options.label: B
options.text: Adding a reranker guarantees zero hallucination in the final generated answer

options.label: C
options.text: Rerankers are only useful when the corpus is small enough to fit in memory

options.label: D
options.text: Rerankers remove the need for an embedding model, simplifying the pipeline

explanation: The standard retrieve-then-rerank pattern exists precisely because fast initial retrieval (bi-encoder/ANN search) trades some precision for speed at corpus scale, while a more expensive cross-encoder reranker can afford to carefully re-score just the small candidate set, improving final precision cheaply. It doesn't replace the embedding model or guarantee hallucination-free output - it improves what reaches the generation step.

title: Q132. Your RAG application allows multi-turn conversation, and a user asks 'What's the deductible for that?' as a follow-up to a prior question about a specific policy. The retrieval step searches using only the literal text 'What's the deductible for that?' and returns irrelevant chunks because 'that' has no resolvable meaning in isolation. What is the most effective fix?
description: Q132. Your RAG application allows multi-turn conversation, and a user asks 'What's the deductible for that?' as a follow-up to a prior question about a specific policy. The retrieval step searches using only the literal text 'What's the deductible for that?' and returns irrelevant chunks because 'that' has no resolvable meaning in isolation. What is the most effective fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a query transformation/rewriting step that uses the conversation history to rewrite the follow-up into a self-contained query (e.g., 'What's the deductible for the homeowners policy P-4471?') before passing it to the retriever

options.label: B
options.text: Disable multi-turn conversation to avoid the ambiguity problem

options.label: C
options.text: Tell users they must phrase every query as a complete, standalone sentence

options.label: D
options.text: Increase the number of retrieved chunks to 100 so the correct one is statistically more likely to appear despite the ambiguous query

explanation: This is the well-known RAG context-resolution problem for conversational queries. Query rewriting/transformation - using conversation history to expand ambiguous follow-ups into self-contained queries before retrieval - directly fixes the root cause, unlike brute-forcing top-k or removing multi-turn support altogether, which sacrifices the feature.

title: Q133. Your team is evaluating two chunk-overlap strategies for a RAG pipeline: 0% overlap between chunks versus 20% overlap. Testing shows the 20%-overlap configuration retrieves more relevant context at the cost of a larger index size and more redundant tokens passed to the LLM. For a use case where the source documents frequently have important context spanning chunk boundaries (e.g., a sentence with a critical caveat starting near the end of one chunk), which configuration better serves the use case, and why?
description: Q133. Your team is evaluating two chunk-overlap strategies for a RAG pipeline: 0% overlap between chunks versus 20% overlap. Testing shows the 20%-overlap configuration retrieves more relevant context at the cost of a larger index size and more redundant tokens passed to the LLM. For a use case where the source documents frequently have important context spanning chunk boundaries (e.g., a sentence with a critical caveat starting near the end of one chunk), which configuration better serves the use case, and why?

options.label: A
options.text: 20% overlap, because the overlap reduces the chance that boundary-spanning context (like a caveat split across two chunks) is lost entirely from both chunks, which is a worthwhile tradeoff against the added storage and token cost for this content type

options.label: B
options.text: 0% overlap, treating a smaller index as generally preferable with little regard for retrieval quality impact
